summary: The parliamentary republic that governed Germany from 1919 to 1933, named for the town where its constitution was drafted following the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II and the 1918-19 German Revolution. Led first by President Friedrich Ebert and later Paul von Hindenburg, it survived left-wing unrest like the Spartacist Uprising and right-wing coups like the Kapp Putsch, but was undermined by the assassination of foreign minister Walter Rathenau, the "stab-in-the-back" myth, and crippling hyperinflation only checked by the Dawes Plan and the Rentenmark. It also fostered the New Objectivity art movement and the Bauhaus before Hindenburg's signing of the Reichstag Fire Decree and the Enabling Act cleared Adolf Hitler's path to dictatorship in 1933.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The republic is most consistently identified as the interwar, post-World War I government of Germany, named for the town of Weimar where its constitution was drafted and ratified in 1919. It is defined in giveaway lines as the parliamentary democracy that preceded, and was undone by, the rise of Nazi Germany, remaining in power until 1933.

clues.clue: Named for the town of Weimar, where its constitution was drafted/ratified in 1919
examples: This government was created after the First World War in the namesake town.
Identify this period of German history that followed World War I and was named for the city where its constitution was written in 1919.

clues.clue: The post-World War I, interwar government of Germany that preceded the rise of Nazi Germany
examples: name this post-World War I government of Germany.
Name this government established after World War I that preceded the rise of Nazi Germany.

clues.clue: A representative democracy that remained in power until 1933, when the Nazis took over
examples: a representative democracy which remained in power until 1933, when the Nazis took over.
This ill-fated German republic which was replaced in 1933 by the Nazi Third Reich.

works.name: Founding: The 1918-19 Revolution & Constitution
works.description: The republic was formed after the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II and the 1918-19 German Revolution ousted the monarchy, with Friedrich Ebert coming to power following the resignation of Chancellor Max von Baden. Ebert's Social Democratic government signed a namesake pact with Army Quartermaster General Wilhelm Groener, guaranteeing military support in part to deal with the left-wing group led by Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht, and it defeated a separate independence attempt in southeastern Germany (Bavaria) led by socialists Eugen Levine and Kurt Eisner.

clues.clue: Formed after the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II and the 1918-19 German Revolution, which ousted the monarchy
examples: This interwar government, formed after the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II, included Paul von Hindenburg as president after 1925.
Identify this government which took over after the 1919 German Revolution ousted the monarchy.

clues.clue: Friedrich Ebert came to power after the resignation of Chancellor Max von Baden
examples: Name this man who came to power following the resignation of Max von Baden.

clues.clue: Ebert signed a namesake pact with Army Quartermaster General Wilhelm Groener, in part to deal with the group led by Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht
examples: This government's first leader signed a namesake pact with Wilhelm Groener, in part to deal with a group led by Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht.
This Social Democrat signed a pact with Army Quartermaster General Wilhelm Groener and fled Berlin during the Kapp Putsch.

clues.clue: Defeated a separate independence attempt in southeastern Germany (Bavaria) led by socialists Eugen Levine and Kurt Eisner
examples: This polity defeated an independence attempt in its south-east led by socialists like Eugen Levine and Kurt Eisner.

works.name: The Spartacist Uprising & Left-Wing Unrest
works.description: The republic's first major crisis was the 1918-19 Spartacist Uprising, a Communist revolt led by Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht's Spartacist League, which published the newspaper The Red Flag; Ebert's government crushed the uprising using the right-wing paramilitary Freikorps, and its leaders were executed. The republic was villainized in right-wing propaganda as having betrayed Germany, and it also faced the Silesian Uprisings, a series of Polish insurrections in the contested territory of Upper Silesia mandated for a plebiscite by the Treaty of Versailles.

clues.clue: Ordered the executions of Karl Liebknecht and Rosa Luxemburg, leaders of the Spartacist Uprising, and was opposed by the Spartacist League
examples: This government ordered the executions of Walter Liebknecht and Rosa Luxemburg, the leaders of the Spartacist Uprising.
it was opposed by the Spartacist League.

clues.clue: Faced/was toppled-at by the Spartacist Uprising, a Communist revolt whose group published the newspaper The Red Flag
examples: it faced a revolution led by the group that published the newspaper The Red Flag.
The Spartacist uprising sought to topple this interwar German government.

clues.clue: Ebert's government crushed the Spartacist Uprising using the right-wing paramilitary Freikorps
examples: The Spartacist League was put down by the right-wing Freikorps on the orders of Friedrich Ebert, who led this government.
While in power during this period, the Social Democratic Party or SPD controversially employed the far-right Freikorps to suppress uprisings by the Spartacus League and the Ruhr Red Army.

clues.clue: Was portrayed as villainous in the "stab-in-the-back" myth (Dolchstoßlegende), which blamed Germany's WWI loss on civilian and Jewish betrayal
examples: This government was portrayed as villainous in the "stab-in-the-back" myth, and it was opposed by the Spartacist League.

clues.clue: Faced the Silesian Uprisings, Polish insurrections in Upper Silesia mandated for a plebiscite by the Treaty of Versailles; it controlled the western part of Upper Silesia
examples: The Silesian Uprisings were a crisis for this German government.
The western part of Upper Silesia was controlled by this government, a representative democracy which remained in power until 1933, when the Nazis took over.

works.name: Right-Wing Coups & Political Violence
works.description: The republic survived the 1920 Kapp Putsch, a right-wing coup defeated by an SPD-called general strike; many of its Freikorps participants later formed the Organisation Consul, a far-right group that threw acid in the face of Philipp Scheidemann and assassinated Jewish foreign minister Walter Rathenau shortly after he negotiated the Treaty of Rapallo with the Soviet Union, as well as finance minister Matthias Erzberger. A separate failed rebellion was put down with the help of Gustav von Kahr.

clues.clue: Was the target of the 1920 Kapp Putsch, a right-wing coup led by Wolfgang Kapp that was defeated by an SPD-called general strike
examples: This government was the target of a coup led by Wolfgang Kapp, and it faced a revolution led by the group that published the newspaper The Red Flag.
This 1920 coup against the Weimar government was supported by the right-wing Freikorps. It was defeated by a general strike, which in turn developed into a left-wing uprising in the Ruhr region.

clues.clue: Many Kapp Putsch participants formed the Organisation Consul, which threw acid in the face of Philipp Scheidemann
examples: Many participants of a coup attempt against this government formed the Organisation Consul, who threw acid in the face of Philipp Schneidemann.

clues.clue: Foreign minister Walter Rathenau was assassinated after signing the Treaty of Rapallo with Russia
examples: This polity's foreign minister Walter Rathenau was assassinated after signing the Treaty of Rapallo with Russia, and one failed rebellion in it was put down with the help of Gustav von Kahr.

clues.clue: A separate failed rebellion within the republic was put down with the help of Gustav von Kahr
examples: one failed rebellion in it was put down with the help of Gustav von Kahr.

works.name: Economic Crisis: Hyperinflation & Recovery
works.description: Between 1921 and 1924, the republic suffered severe hyperinflation, caused in part by the Treaty of Versailles's reparations (a $5 billion payment) and the Allied occupation of the Ruhr; the crisis ended after the introduction of the Rentenmark. Its adoption of the Dawes Plan failed to fully stabilize the economy in the longer term, leaving the republic economically vulnerable heading into the Great Depression.

clues.clue: Suffered severe hyperinflation from 1921 to 1924, caused in part by Treaty of Versailles reparations, which ended after the introduction of the Rentenmark
examples: From 1921 to 1924, the Weimar Republic underwent this economic phenomenon, caused in part by the Treaty of Versailles. It ended after the introduction of the Rentenmark.
This government introduced the Rentenmark to counteract hyperinflation, and its presidents were Friedrich Ebert and Paul von Hindenburg.

clues.clue: Adopted the Dawes Plan, which failed to save it from massive hyperinflation
examples: This government's adoption of the Dawes Plan failed to save it from massive hyperinflation.

clues.clue: Had to deal with hyperinflation and the payment of $5 billion in war reparations, alongside the Allied occupation of the Ruhr
examples: In power until the rise of the Third Reich, it had to deal with hyperinflation and the payment of $5 billion in war reparations.
This person's tenure saw the Allied occupation of the Ruhr and a severe bout of hyperinflation.

works.name: Presidents: Friedrich Ebert & Paul von Hindenburg
works.description: Friedrich Ebert, a Social Democrat, served as the republic's first president and Germany's first democratically elected leader; he was succeeded by the aging Field Marshal Paul von Hindenburg. Hindenburg's later presidency saw him appoint Franz von Papen as vice-chancellor to try to moderate Hitler, and ultimately sign both the Reichstag Fire Decree and the Enabling Act, clearing the path for Hitler's dictatorship.

clues.clue: Friedrich Ebert was the republic's first president, later succeeded by Paul von Hindenburg
examples: Friedrich Ebert was the first president of this republic, which was established following World War I. This republic was later also led by Paul von Hindenburg.
Name this first person who was succeeded as President of his country by Paul von Hindenburg.

clues.clue: Franz von Papen was appointed vice-chancellor by Hindenburg to try to moderate Hitler
examples: This vice-chancellor, appointed by Hindenburg to moderate Hitler, lost power in the German government after the Night of the Long Knives.

clues.clue: Kurt von Schleicher was the republic's last chancellor, replaced by Hitler at Hindenburg's decision after earning the hatred of Franz von Papen
examples: This last chancellor of the Weimar Republic tried to rein in the Nazi Party but earned the hatred of Franz von Papen, who persuaded Hindenburg to replace this man with Hitler.

works.name: Cultural Life & Legacy
works.description: The republic's cultural era fostered the New Objectivity (Neue Sachlichkeit) art movement, later dismissed by the Nazis as "degenerate art," as well as the Bauhaus school; its instability is depicted in literature such as Hans Fallada's novel Little Man, What Now? and Christopher Isherwood's stories adapted into the play I Am a Camera. Its constitutional era ended with the Reichstag Fire in February 1933, allegedly set by Marinus van der Lubbe and blamed by the Nazis on the Communists, and the subsequent Enabling Act, which let the Nazi-controlled Cabinet pass laws without the Reichstag.

clues.clue: Fostered the New Objectivity art movement and the Bauhaus school during its cultural era
examples: The New Objectivity movement and Bauhaus flourished during the cultural era defined by this regime.
Name this period, during which the New Objectivity movement developed.

clues.clue: Its art (including New Objectivity works) was later classified as "degenerate art" by its Nazi political successors
examples: In the period following it, many of its artworks were classified as "degenerate art" by its political successors.

clues.clue: Its instability is depicted in Hans Fallada's novel Little Man, What Now?, and its tenure is depicted in Christopher Isherwood's stories adapted into the play I Am a Camera
examples: Name this government, which is in power during the action of the novel Little Man, What Now? The play I Am A Camera adapted stories set during this government's tenure by Christopher Isherwood.

clues.clue: Ended with the February 1933 Reichstag Fire, allegedly set by Marinus van der Lubbe and blamed by the Nazis on the Communists
examples: The last president of this government, Paul von Hindenburg, passed the Reichstag Fire Decree, which assisted in the rise of Adolf Hitler to power.

clues.clue: Effectively ended with the passage of the 1933 Enabling Act, which let the Nazi-controlled Cabinet violate the constitution and pass laws without the Reichstag
examples: The Weimar Republic basically came to an end with the passage of this 1933 law, which allowed the Nazi-controlled Cabinet to violate the Constitution and pass laws without consulting the Reichstag.
This interwar government of Germany that was ended when Paul von Hindenburg signed the Enabling Act.

comprehensive_summary: The Weimar Republic (1919-1933) was the parliamentary republic that governed Germany between the two world wars, named for the town of Weimar where its constitution was drafted and ratified in 1919. It was formed after the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II and the 1918-19 German Revolution ousted the German monarchy, with Friedrich Ebert coming to power following the resignation of Chancellor Max von Baden; Ebert's Social Democratic government signed a namesake pact with Army Quartermaster General Wilhelm Groener, guaranteeing military support in part to counter the left-wing group led by Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht, and it defeated a separate socialist independence attempt in southeastern Germany (Bavaria) led by Eugen Levine and Kurt Eisner. The republic's first major crisis was the Spartacist Uprising, a Communist revolt led by Luxemburg and Liebknecht's Spartacist League (publisher of the newspaper The Red Flag); Ebert's government crushed the uprising using the right-wing paramilitary Freikorps and ordered the executions of its leaders, while right-wing propaganda villainized the republic in the "stab-in-the-back" myth (Dolchstoßlegende), which falsely blamed Germany's WWI defeat on civilian and Jewish betrayal. The republic also had to manage the Silesian Uprisings, a series of Polish insurrections in the contested territory of Upper Silesia (whose western part it controlled) mandated for a plebiscite by the Treaty of Versailles. It survived the 1920 Kapp Putsch, a right-wing coup led by Wolfgang Kapp that was defeated by an SPD-called general strike; many of its Freikorps participants later formed the Organisation Consul, a far-right group that threw acid in the face of Philipp Scheidemann and assassinated the Jewish foreign minister Walter Rathenau shortly after he negotiated the Treaty of Rapallo with the Soviet Union, as well as finance minister Matthias Erzberger. A separate failed rebellion was put down with the help of Gustav von Kahr. Economically, the republic suffered severe hyperinflation from 1921 to 1924, driven in part by Treaty of Versailles reparations (a $5 billion payment) and the Allied occupation of the Ruhr; the crisis ended with the introduction of the Rentenmark, though the republic's adoption of the Dawes Plan failed to secure lasting stability. Friedrich Ebert, the republic's first president and Germany's first democratically elected leader, was succeeded by the aging Field Marshal Paul von Hindenburg, who later appointed Franz von Papen as vice-chancellor in an attempt to moderate Hitler and saw Kurt von Schleicher serve as the republic's last chancellor before Papen persuaded Hindenburg to replace Schleicher with Hitler. Culturally, the republic's era fostered the New Objectivity (Neue Sachlichkeit) art movement — later dismissed by the Nazis as "degenerate art" — and the Bauhaus school, and its instability is depicted in literature such as Hans Fallada's novel Little Man, What Now? and Christopher Isherwood's stories, adapted into the play I Am a Camera. The republic's constitutional order effectively ended with the February 1933 Reichstag Fire, allegedly set by Marinus van der Lubbe and blamed by the Nazis on the Communists, after which Hindenburg signed the Reichstag Fire Decree; its final collapse came with the passage of the 1933 Enabling Act, which allowed the Nazi-controlled Cabinet to violate the constitution and pass laws without the Reichstag, ending the republic and clearing the way for Adolf Hitler's dictatorship.
recursive_suggestions: Excluded: 10 of 13 tossups fetched under this search (T1-T6, T8, T9, T11, T12) had their actual answer as 'Germany' or 'Federal Republic of Germany,' not 'Weimar Republic' — these were broad country tossups spanning many eras (colonial Namibia, WWI chemical warfare, Nazi-era physics emigration, 19th-century historians like Ranke/Mommsen/Hegel, German Expressionist cinema like Caligari/M/Fritz Lang) and were not used as this topic's own clue content, even though several accept 'Weimar Republic' as an alternate answer

New Objectivity (Neue Sachlichkeit) — recurring Fine Arts movement/answerline (Otto Dix, George Grosz, Christian Schad, Max Beckmann) that flourished during and satirized this government; belongs on its own Fine Arts topic page rather than folded into this History page
Spartacist Uprising / Spartacist League — recurring independent answerline (Rosa Luxemburg, Karl Liebknecht); has enough independent bonus-part presence to eventually merit its own topic
Friedrich Ebert — recurring individual figure answerline (3x own bonus-part answer); could merit a standalone figure page
Rosa Luxemburg — recurring individual figure answerline (2x own bonus-part answer)
Kapp Putsch — recurring independent event answerline; currently folded into this page's right-wing coups section
Walter Rathenau — recurring individual figure answerline; assassinated foreign minister, currently folded into this page

Fritz Lang / German Expressionist cinema — distinct Fine Arts/Film topic (Metropolis, M, The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari) that arose during the Weimar era but was answered as 'Federal Republic of Germany' or 'Germany' in these questions
